note_4_5_0.js.title: Avoid 'producing' energy
note_4_5_0.js.text: Students often incorrectly write that photosynthesis 'produces' or 'creates' energy. Remember that energy cannot be created; always state that light energy is *converted* or *transferred* into chemical energy.

note_4_5_1.js.title: Excitation vs Ionisation
note_4_5_1.js.text: Do not confuse the excitation of electrons with ionisation. Photoionisation occurs only when the electrons have gained enough energy to completely leave the chlorophyll molecule.

note_4_5_2.js.title: NADP vs NAD
note_4_5_2.js.text: Always write 'reduced NADP' (or NADPH) for photosynthesis. Using 'reduced NAD' will lose you marks, as NAD is specific to respiration.

note_4_5_3.js.title: Limiting factor definition
note_4_5_3.js.text: A limiting factor is the one factor that is at the lowest or least favourable value. Don't just say 'it limits the rate'; specify that it is the factor restricting any further increase in the rate.

note_4_5_4.js.title: Absorption vs Action Spectra
note_4_5_4.js.text: Be prepared to overlay these graphs. If the peaks of the absorption spectrum perfectly align with the action spectrum, it provides evidence that those specific pigments are responsible for photosynthesis.

note_4_5_5.js.title: Units of Productivity
note_4_5_5.js.text: Do not forget the units for GPP and NPP! They must include an area and time component, usually written as kJ m⁻² yr⁻¹.

note_4_5_6.js.title: Measuring Oxygen
note_4_5_6.js.text: Counting bubbles is highly inaccurate because bubble sizes vary. Examiners prefer methods that measure the *volume* of oxygen gas collected using a gas syringe or a photosynthometer.

note_4_5_7.js.title: Pencil, not Pen
note_4_5_7.js.text: Always state that the origin line must be drawn in pencil. Ink contains separate pigments that would dissolve in the solvent and run up the chromatography paper, invalidating results.

note_4_5_8.js.title: ATP is a Nucleotide Derivative
note_4_5_8.js.text: Remember that ATP is structurally similar to an RNA nucleotide, but it contains three phosphate groups instead of one, and its base is always adenine.

note_4_5_9.js.title: Products of Phosphorylation
note_4_5_9.js.text: Cyclic photophosphorylation ONLY produces ATP. Non-cyclic produces both ATP AND reduced NADP, as well as oxygen from the photolysis of water.

note_4_5_10.js.title: RuBisCO vs RuBP
note_4_5_10.js.text: Do not mix up the enzyme and the substrate! RuBisCO is the enzyme that catalyses the reaction; RuBP (Ribulose bisphosphate) is the 5-carbon substrate it acts upon.

note_4_5_11.js.title: Palisade vs Spongy Mesophyll
note_4_5_11.js.text: Be precise: palisade cells are adapted for maximum light absorption (packed with chloroplasts), whereas spongy mesophyll cells are adapted for gas exchange (air spaces).

note_4_5_12.js.title: Direction of Arrows
note_4_5_12.js.text: In a food web or chain, arrows do NOT mean 'is eaten by'. The arrows strictly represent the direction of energy transfer between trophic levels.

note_4_5_13.js.title: Why energy is lost
note_4_5_13.js.text: Never say energy is 'used for respiration'. Energy is *released* by respiration and lost as heat. Energy is also lost through uneaten parts (bones) and excretion (urine/faeces).

note_4_5_14.js.title: The NPP Equation
note_4_5_14.js.text: NPP = GPP - R. Always state clearly that 'R' stands for respiratory losses by the *plants* (producers), not the animals that eat them.

note_4_5_15.js.title: Decomposition releases CO₂
note_4_5_15.js.text: Students often forget exactly how decomposition returns CO₂ to the atmosphere. It is because the saprobionts (bacteria/fungi) secrete enzymes, absorb the products, and then *respire* them.

note_4_5_16.js.title: Pioneer Species
note_4_5_16.js.text: Pioneer species (like lichens) alter the harsh abiotic conditions, making them less hostile to allow new, larger species to colonise. The new species then *outcompete* the pioneers.

note_4_5_17.js.title: Dendrochronology & Peat Bogs
note_4_5_17.js.text: When discussing evidence for past climates, remember that wider tree rings indicate warmer/wetter conditions, and peat bogs preserve pollen due to anaerobic and acidic conditions.

note_4_5_18.js.title: Extrapolation Limitations
note_4_5_18.js.text: Examiners will test you on the reliability of data. Extrapolating to predict future climates is unreliable because it assumes current trends continue unchanged and ignores future emissions policies.

note_4_5_19.js.title: The Predator-Prey Lag Phase
note_4_5_19.js.text: In predator-prey graphs, the predator population peaks *after* the prey population peaks. Always mention the time lag required for predators to reproduce after eating the abundant prey.

note_4_5_20.js.title: Random vs Systematic Sampling
note_4_5_20.js.text: Use a random number generator for quadrats in a uniform area to avoid bias. Only use a transect (systematic sampling) if there is an environmental gradient like a shoreline or forest edge.

note_4_5_21.js.title: Denaturation is not 'killing'
note_4_5_21.js.text: Never say high temperatures 'kill' the enzyme. State that extreme kinetic energy breaks the hydrogen and ionic bonds, altering the tertiary structure so the active site is no longer complementary.

note_4_5_22.js.title: Q10 Coefficient
note_4_5_22.js.text: You may be asked to calculate Q10. Remember it represents the factor by which the rate of reaction increases for every 10°C rise in temperature. A Q10 of 2 means the rate doubles.

note_4_5_23.js.title: Mutations are Random
note_4_5_23.js.text: A common mistake is saying 'the environment caused a mutation to help them survive'. No! Mutations occur randomly. The environment simply provides the selection pressure that gives the surviving allele an advantage.

note_4_5_24.js.title: Allopatric vs Sympatric
note_4_5_24.js.text: Allopatric = 'Different country' (Geographical isolation). Sympatric = 'Same country' (Reproductive isolation, such as different mating seasons or behaviors, within the same area).

note_4_5_25.js.title: Peer Review is Crucial
note_4_5_25.js.text: Whenever asked how scientific data is validated, the standard marking points are always 'peer review', 'published in scientific journals', and 'repeated by independent scientific bodies'.

note_4_5_26.js.title: Conservation vs Preservation
note_4_5_26.js.text: Conservation involves the *active management* and intervention of an ecosystem to maintain biodiversity, whereas preservation simply means leaving the ecosystem entirely alone.

note_4_6_0.js.title: Sterilisation Etiquette
note_4_6_0.js.text: Pass the inoculating loop through a roaring Bunsen flame until red hot. Do NOT place it down on the bench to cool; hold it near the flame to prevent contamination from the air.

note_4_6_1.js.title: Turbidity limitations
note_4_6_1.js.text: Using a colorimeter to measure turbidity (cloudiness) gives a total cell count, which includes both living and dead cells. Only a serial dilution and plating will give a viable (living) cell count.

note_4_6_2.js.title: Logarithmic Scales
note_4_6_2.js.text: Bacterial growth graphs often use a log₁₀ scale on the y-axis because the numbers get so large so quickly. Pay close attention to the axes before reading off a value.

note_4_6_3.js.title: Log Phase = Exponential Growth
note_4_6_3.js.text: During the exponential (log) phase, there are no limiting factors. Nutrients are abundant and waste levels are low, allowing the bacteria to divide at their maximum theoretical rate.

note_4_6_4.js.title: Viruses are Not Cells
note_4_6_4.js.text: Never refer to a virus as a cell. They do not have a plasma membrane, cytoplasm, or ribosomes. They only consist of genetic material enclosed in a protein coat (capsid).

note_4_6_5.js.title: HIV targets specific cells
note_4_6_5.js.text: State clearly that HIV targets T-helper cells (because they possess the CD4 receptor). The destruction of T-helper cells restricts both the humoral (B-cell) and cell-mediated (T-killer) immune responses.

note_4_6_6.js.title: Lysozyme is an Enzyme
note_4_6_6.js.text: Lysozyme is found in tears, saliva, and sweat. It works by breaking down the peptidoglycan cell walls of bacteria. It is NOT an antibody and it is a non-specific defence.

note_4_6_7.js.title: Inflammation mechanism
note_4_6_7.js.text: Inflammation causes swelling, but not because 'cells get bigger'. Histamine makes the capillary walls more permeable, allowing blood plasma (tissue fluid) to leak out into the surrounding tissue.

note_4_6_8.js.title: Who makes the antibodies?
note_4_6_8.js.text: B cells do not directly 'shoot' antibodies at pathogens. They must first undergo clonal expansion and differentiate into plasma cells. It is the plasma cells that secrete the antibodies.

note_4_6_9.js.title: Antibody Structure
note_4_6_9.js.text: Don't forget the disulfide bridges! When describing antibody structure, always mention the four polypeptide chains (two heavy, two light) held together by disulfide bonds, featuring a specific variable region.

note_4_6_10.js.title: Passive Immunity is Temporary
note_4_6_10.js.text: Passive immunity (like maternal antibodies in breast milk) provides immediate protection, but it does NOT create memory cells. The antibodies will eventually break down, leaving no long-term immunity.

note_4_6_11.js.title: Bacteriostatic vs Bacteriocidal
note_4_6_11.js.text: Bacteriostatic antibiotics do not kill bacteria; they inhibit their growth/reproduction. Always add that this gives the host's own immune system time to destroy the bacteria.

note_4_6_12.js.title: The Bacteria are Resistant
note_4_6_12.js.text: Never write that the 'patient has become resistant' or 'immune' to the antibiotic. It is the *bacteria* that have acquired the resistance allele allowing them to survive the antibiotic.

note_4_6_13.js.title: Comparing Inhibition Zones
note_4_6_13.js.text: A larger zone of inhibition (clear area) indicates the antibiotic is more effective at killing the bacteria—but remember this assumes both antibiotics diffuse through the agar at the same rate.

note_4_6_14.js.title: Autolysis vs Putrefaction
note_4_6_14.js.text: Make sure you distinguish them clearly. Autolysis is the self-digestion of tissue by its own intracellular enzymes inside lysosomes. Putrefaction follows this, caused by the hydrolytic enzymes of external bacteria.

note_4_6_15.js.title: Cooling Curves vary
note_4_6_15.js.text: Body temperature alone is not enough to accurately determine Time of Death. Examiners want you to list factors that alter the cooling rate, such as ambient temperature, body mass, or clothing.

note_4_6_16.js.title: Gel Electrophoresis Speed
note_4_6_16.js.text: In gel electrophoresis, smaller/shorter DNA fragments travel faster and further down the agarose gel towards the positive anode than larger fragments do.
